title: US Firms Suffer as Small Businesses Thrive under Trump's Tariff Policy: Winners and Losers Revealed
body:
As the world marks one year since the implementation of sweeping tariffs by the US, a major shift in global trade flows has become evident. Announced by former US President Donald Trump on April 2, 2025, as a move towards "economic independence," the tariffs have significantly altered the dynamics of international trade. With trade data now available, it has become clear which countries have benefited and which have lost out under this new trade regime.

One of the key beneficiaries of Trump's tariff policy has been Vietnam. The Southeast Asian nation has seen a substantial increase in its exports to the US, with American companies turning to Vietnam as a viable alternative to Chinese suppliers. This shift has been largely driven by the US tariffs imposed on Chinese goods, which have made Vietnamese products more competitive in the American market. As a result, Vietnam's trade surplus with the US has grown significantly, with the country's exports to the US increasing by over 20% in the past year.

In contrast, several countries have been negatively impacted by the US tariffs. Mexico, for instance, has seen a decline in its exports to the US, with the country's trade deficit with its northern neighbor widening significantly. The tariffs have also hit hard on several European countries, including Germany and France, which have been forced to re-evaluate their trade strategies with the US. According to trade data, Europe's exports to the US have fallen by over 15% in the past year, with several key industries, including the automotive and aerospace sectors, being particularly hard hit.

Meanwhile, countries such as China, which initially bore the brunt of Trump's trade war, have begun to adapt to the new trade environment. While the US tariffs have imposed significant costs on Chinese exporters, the country has managed to diversify its trade base and find new markets for its goods. According to trade experts, China's exports to countries such as Brazil and India have increased significantly in recent months, helping to mitigate the impact of the US tariffs.

The tariffs have also had a profound impact on the US itself, with American companies and consumers footing the bill for the protectionist measures. According to estimates, the tariffs have added billions of dollars to the cost of goods imported into the US, with many American businesses struggling to absorb the increased costs. As a result, there are growing calls for the US to reassess its trade policies and seek more collaborative approaches to resolving trade disputes.

As the world adjusts to the new trade landscape, one thing is clear: the impact of Trump's tariffs will be felt for years to come. While some countries have managed to adapt and even thrive in this new environment, others have been left struggling to cope with the consequences of the US trade policy. As the US looks to the future, it will be crucial for policymakers to consider the lessons of the past year and seek more inclusive and collaborative approaches to trade that benefit all nations involved.
